text:
You are building an admin product catalog page for an e-commerce team.

The catalog is read-heavy and should feel fast for day-to-day browsing, so avoid re-querying product data on every request.

Admins can trigger a "Sync latest catalog" action from the page when upstream ERP/PIM data changes (pricing, inventory, availability). After submitting, they should be able to continue working immediately, even if the product list is briefly stale.

The expected behavior is that product data is refreshed in the background and becomes up to date shortly after, across any views that depend on the same catalog data.

Implement the sync trigger as a regular HTML form that uses an inline Server Action in the page.
Use the cache tag name "products" consistently for catalog caching and invalidation.

In practice, sync jobs can touch thousands of SKUs, so operators prioritize a responsive admin experience and eventual consistency across catalog views over forcing every request to block on freshly recomputed data.
I’m going to inspect the app structure to find the catalog page, current caching, and any existing server actions so I can wire the sync flow into the right place without breaking the read path.

text: I found a very small Next app, so I’m checking the page and config directly. Next step is to verify whether it’s already using App Router server components and whether cache tagging is available in this version.

I’ve confirmed the app is basically a blank Next App Router shell with a mocked product source. I’m checking the current Next caching/server-action APIs from the official docs so I can use the right tag-based invalidation pattern instead of guessing.

I have enough context to patch this now. I’m updating the app to use a cached catalog snapshot tagged with `products`, then wiring the page’s form to an inline Server Action that invalidates that tag with stale-while-revalidate semantics.
I’ve got the right invalidation behavior from the docs. I’m tightening the implementation to use the new cache tagging API directly and then I’ll run a build to catch any Next/TS issues.

text:
Implemented the catalog page with cached reads and a non-blocking sync action.

What changed:
- Added a catalog snapshot fetch in [`/home/user/workspace/app/page.tsx`](./home/user/workspace/app/page.tsx) using `use cache` plus `cacheTag('products')`.
- Added an inline Server Action in the same page that runs from a regular HTML `<form>` and calls `revalidateTag('products', 'max')`, so admins can continue working while the catalog refreshes in the background.
- Kept the cache tag name consistent as `products` for both caching and invalidation.
- Enabled cache components in [`/home/user/workspace/next.config.ts`](./home/user/workspace/next.config.ts).

Verification:
- `npm run build` passes.

One incidental change:
- Next updated [`/home/user/workspace/tsconfig.json`](./home/user/workspace/tsconfig.json) during the build to include `.next/dev/types/**/*.ts`.

Docs I used:
- [Next.js `cacheTag`](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag)
- [Next.js `revalidateTag`](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag)

If you want, I can also split the styling into a CSS module or hook this up to a real product backend instead of the mocked data source.
